Defendant City And County Of San Francisco'S Demurrer To Amended Petition For Writ Of Mandate/ Prohibition/ Certification. (Part 2 Of Tentative Ruling Only)
Off Calendar Mar-21-2019 Off Calendar

DEMURRER TO AMENDED PETITION FOR WRIT OF MANDATE/ PROHIBITION/ CERTIFICATION
Off Calendar Mar-21-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances:
Trevor Koski, esq. (415-554-4223) Deputy City Attorney of City and County of San Francisco for Defendant.
No other appearances.

Not argued, Counsel presents proposed order. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO's DEMURRER TO PETITIONERS' amended verified petition.

CCSF's demurrer to Plaintiff Lawrence Kempinski's first amended verified petition for writ of mandate for violation of Gov't Code 3304(b) is sustained without leave to amend. (See Code Civ. Proc. 430.10(e).) (see word minutes for full details).

Order is signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Not Reported. =(302/EPS)

DEMURRER TO PETITION FOR WRIT OF MANDATE/ PROHIBITION/ CERTIFICATION
Off Calendar Nov-13-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

RESPONDENT/DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S DEMURRER TO PETITIONER'S VERIFIED PETITION.

Respondent City and County of San Francisco's demurrer to the sole cause of action alleged by the three Retired Officers in their petition for writ of mandate for violation of Government Code 3304(b) is sustained with 20 days leave to amend. If they can do so in good faith, the Retired Officers have leave to allege specific facts that the administrative appeal afforded by the Police Commission was futile and did not comport with due process and/or the requirements of section 3304(b). For purposes of this demurrer, the court assumes that the Chief's decision to file a disciplinary complaint against the retired officers is "punitive action" within the meaning of section 3304(b). The petitioner and the judicially noticeable facts show that the Police Commission provides a comprehensive quasi-judicial hearing process to determine whether the Chief's complaint has merit and whether an officer should be disciplined. That detailed process presumptively comports with due process and section 3304(b). The fact that the Police Commission's process during the timeframe applicable to Retired Officers erroneously did not allow for a writ to be taken to the superior court after the Commission issued its decision by itself does not render the process fatally defective in these particular circumstances because Retired Officers retired before any sort of hearing occurred. It is quite possible that the Commission could have rejected the Chief's disciplinary complaints. Moreover, the Retired Officers could have sought pre-hearing relief to vindicate their POBRA rights with regard to the lack of writ relief or other perceived problems with the Police Commission's hearing process. (Alameida v. State Personnel Bd. (2004) 120 Cal.App.4th 46, 54; Mounger v. Gates (1987) 193 Cal.App.3d 1248, 1256-1257.)

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Harold Kahn, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/HK)
